subject: math
topic_title: Statistics — Collection, Tabulation and Ungrouped Data
source_title: New General Mathematics SSS 1 by M.F. Macrae et al
learning_objectives: Define statistics and understand types of data (quantitative and qualitative, discrete and continuous)
Collect and present data in a frequency distribution table using tally marks
Calculate the range of a set of data
Calculate the mean (arithmetic mean) of ungrouped data
Find the median of ungrouped data
Find the mode of ungrouped data

sections.heading: Introduction to Statistics and Types of Data
sections.content: Statistics is the collection, presentation and interpretation of data. Data can be classified as: Quantitative (numerical): (a) Discrete — obtained by counting (e.g., number of students, cars), always a whole number; (b) Continuous — obtained by measurement (e.g., height, weight, age), can be decimal. Qualitative (categorical): describes quality (e.g., colour, taste, make of product). Raw data is data not yet arranged in order. Data is presented using frequency distribution tables with columns for variable (x), tally, and frequency (f).

sections.heading: Frequency Distribution Tables
sections.content: To prepare a frequency table: (1) list all possible values in the variable column; (2) go through the data and make tally marks (a bundle = 5 strokes); (3) count the tallies for each frequency. Example: marks scored in a test are listed, and the frequency table shows each mark value, its tally, and its frequency count.

sections.heading: Range
sections.content: The range is the difference between the largest and smallest values in a data set. Example: for scores 79, 60, 52, 34, 58, 60 — arranged in order: 79, 60, 60, 58, 52, 34 — range = 79 − 34 = 45.

sections.heading: Mean (Arithmetic Mean)
sections.content: The mean = sum of all values ÷ number of values. For frequency tables: mean = Σ(f × x) ÷ Σf. Example: scores 2,3,4,5,6,7 with frequencies 2,4,7,2,3,2 → Σf = 20; Σfx = 4+12+28+10+18+14 = 86; mean = 86/20 = 4.3.

sections.heading: Median
sections.content: The median is the middle value when data is arranged in order. For an odd number of values (n), the median is the ((n+1)/2)th value. For an even number of values, it is the average of the (n/2)th and (n/2 + 1)th values. Example: for 21 students' temperatures, with 1+2+7 = 10 results up to 80°C, the 11th result falls in the 81°C group, so median = 81°C.

sections.heading: Mode
sections.content: The mode is the value with the highest frequency (appears most often). A data set can be unimodal (one mode), bimodal (two modes) or multimodal (more than two modes). Example: in temperatures 78(×1), 79(×2), 80(×7), 81(×5)..., the mode = 80°C (highest frequency of 7).
